q:
Throwing balls in the air
Pairs of students stand in two rows separated by four meters and face each other. Each pair of students has a large ball. The students pass the balls from their chest to their partners’ chest, as shown. A video camera is used to film this interaction. When viewing the video, the students note that the highest point in the trajectories of all the balls are nearly the same height above the ground. Which of the following claims is supported by this evidence?
o: The students threw the ball to their partner with nearly the same speed.
The students threw the ball to their partner at nearly the same angle to the horizontal.
The time the ball is in the air is nearly the same for each ball.
It is impossible for the balls to not go to the same height, because the distance between each pair of students is the same.
Each ball must have the same mass.

q: 929 Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight. A group of students is studying the two-dimensional motion of a sphere as it moves through the air. They have a spring-loaded launcher, which always launches the sphere with the same unknown speed v 0 . The students wish to determine v 0 experimentally. To do this, they have access only to the items shown in Figure 1 . Besides the sphere and the launcher, the items are a 60 -centimeter-tall stand on which the launcher can be mounted horizontally and moved vertically to different heights, a meterstick, several sheets of plain paper, and several sheets of carbon paper. Whenever the sphere bounces off of the carbon paper, as shown in Figure 2 , the carbon paper produces a visible mark on the sheet of plain paper that is under the carbon paper. (a) i. Indicate quantities that the students could measure that would allow them to determine the speed v 0 at which the launcher launches the sphere by using a linear graph. ii. Briefly describe a method to reduce experimental uncertainty. (b) i. Identify which quantities the students could graph on the horizontal and vertical axes to create a linear graph that could be used to determine v 0 . ii. Briefly describe the relationship between v 0 and one feature of the graph. Your answer may include an equation that relates v 0 to this feature of the graph. Later, the students analyze their data and determine that v 0 = 4.1 m / . Using the same launcher as in part (a) the students set up the experiment shown in Figure 3 . They disagree on the formula relating the horizontal distance D that the sphere travels to the angle θ L above the horizontal it is launched from. Students 1 and 2 make the following claims. Student 1 : “I found this formula for D on the Internet: D = v 0 2 g sin ( 2 θ L ) .” Student 2 : “I think there is an error in that formula. I would expect D to be proportional to sin ( θ L ) , not sin ( 2 θ L ) .” To determine who is correct, they write the following mathematical model: D = v 0 2 g sin ( n θ L ) , where n is a positive constant and may or may not be an integer. The students wish to determine n experimentally. Using the set-up shown in Figure 3 , the students measure values of D for different launch angles θ L , producing the data shown in the following table. θ L ( deg. ) D ( m ) 2 0.335 10 0.690 18 1.070 24 1.320 30 1.520 (c) i. Label the axes of the grid provided with measured or calculated quantities. Include units, as appropriate. The graphed quantities should yield a linear graph that can be used to determine an experimental value for n . ii. On the grid create a graph of the quantities indicated in part c (i). Label the vertical and horizontal axes with numerical scales. Plot the corresponding data points on the grid. Table 1 is provided in your booklet for scratch work and will not be scored. iii. Draw a best-fit line for the data plotted in part c (ii). (d) Using the best-fit line that you drew in part (c)(iii) and the launch speed v 0 = 4.1 m / , calculate an experimental value for n .

q: Read each part carefully. Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight. A sphere is launched from below a horizontal surface at an unknown angle θ above the horizontal, as shown. It exits the launcher at the surface level. The launcher can be adjusted to give the sphere different launch speeds. Students are to perform an experiment to determine θ using the setup shown. They are expected to find θ by plotting their data in a graph. The students have access to equipment commonly found in a high school physics laboratory, but the experiment must use the setup shown. Assume that the effects of air resistance on the sphere are negligible. (a) Briefly identify each quantity to be measured, the symbol used to represent that quantity, and the equipment that would be used to measure the quantity. (b) Briefly describe the procedure to be used to determine the angle θ . Provide enough detail so that another student could replicate the experiment, including any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty. As needed, use the symbols defined in part (a). (c) Which quantities (raw data or calculated from the data) would be graphed on the vertical and horizontal axes to produce a graph that could be used to determine the angle θ ? (d) Describe which information from the graph described in part (c) would be used and how it would be used to determine the angle θ . (e) Another group of students perform the same experiment, and they use a sphere with the same diameter as the first group’s but with a larger mass. If the second group follows the same procedure and analysis as described in part (b) through part (d), will the value of the angle θ obtained with the experiment that uses a heavier sphere be greater than, less than, or equal to the first group’s result from using a sphere that has less mass?
